turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 24-20, Fuld versus Palestine Liberation Organization and the consolidated case. Before us are two lawsuits against the Palestine Liberation Organization, the PLO, and the Palestinian Authority, the PA. Entities responsible for carrying out governmental functions in parts of the West Bank and Gaza Strip. While other countries recognize the PLO and the PA as sovereign, the United States does not. In fact, the United States has for decades restricted their activities on US soil in part out of concerns over their support for acts of overseas terrorism. As part of the federal government's wider legal response to international terrorism, Congress enacted the Anti-Terrorism Act of 1990, the ATA, which creates a civil damages remedy, including for aiding and abetting liability in federal court for any US national killed or injured by an act of overseas terrorism. In the cases before us, plaintiffs injured or killed by such acts brought ATA suits in Federal District Dourt in New York against the PLO and the PA. Both sets of plaintiffs asserted that the District Courts had personal jurisdiction over those entities. Personal jurisdiction is a term of art, it refers to a court's power to hear a case with regard to a particular individual or entity. As the basis for personal jurisdiction, the plaintiff cited the promoting security and justice for Victims of Terrorism Act, what I'll call the Promoting Security and Justice Act for short. That act refers to the PLO and the PA by name and states that they "shall be deemed to have consented to personal jurisdiction" in ATA cases in two enumerated circumstances. The first is when the PLO and the PA make payments to people imprisoned for committing an act of terrorism that injured or killed an American. The second is when those two entities undertake certain specified activities on US soil. The trial courts found that the evidence showed that the PLO and the PA had engaged in conduct sufficient to satisfy at least the first prong, but both courts went on to hold that the assertion of personal jurisdiction under the Promoting Security and Justice Act was unconstitutional. The Court of Appeals agreed and we granted review at the behest of the plaintiffs and the United States, which had intervened to defend the law's constitutionality. The requirement that a court have personal jurisdiction over a defendant, we have said, flows from the due process clause. Our modern personal jurisdiction cases have considered only the limitations imposed by the Fourteenth Amendment on the authority of state courts. Those standards derived from a 1945 case known as International Shoe versus Washington, and have been reviewed in many subsequent cases over the decades. As every first year law student learns, in order for a state court to exercise personal jurisdiction over a defendant consistent with the Fourteenth Amendment, the defendant must have minimum contacts with the forum state. That means he must have taken some act to purposefully avail himself of the privilege of conducting activities in that state, that ensures that maintaining suit is reasonable and it does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice to hail him into the courts of that particular state. We have repeatedly emphasized that these standards are driven by two related but distinguishable principles, treating defendants fairly and protecting interstate federalism. In the Fourteenth Amendment context, the later concern, interstate federalism recognizes that the Fourteenth Amendment's limits on personal jurisdiction are important to the orderly administration of laws in our federal system of government. In that system, the sovereign authority of each state is bounded by that state's borders and the limitations on personal jurisdiction imposed by the Fourteenth Amendment help ensure that the states do not through their courts exercise authority beyond those territorial limitations. The PLO and the PA urge us to apply this familiar Fourteenth Amendment framework here. We decline to do so. The language of the Fifth at Fourteenth Amendments is almost identical, but the Fifth Amendment applies to the federal government and the Fourteenth to the states. That distinction makes a difference. The federal government, unlike the states, has exclusive and unique powers in the international sphere. While the authority of each state is bounded by its geographical borders, there is no equivalent imaginary border around the exterior of the United States. Given the distinct territorial reach of the federal government's sovereign power, we hold that the limitations imposed by the Fourteenth Amendment on the authority of state courts are not applicable to the federal government. Rather, the Fifth Amendment permits a more flexible jurisdictional inquiry, commensurate with the federal government's broader sovereign authority. Now, we do not, however, determine definitively in these cases the outer bounds of the federal government's power to hail foreign defendants into US courts. The private petitioners argue that the Fifth Amendment imposes no territorial limits on personal jurisdiction. The United States asks us not to embrace, at least for now, this maximalist theory. Because the Promoting Security and Justice Act ties federal jurisdiction to conduct closely related to the United States that implicates important and ongoing foreign policy concerns, we agree with the government that we may leave this broader theory for another day. Whatever the outer limits of the Fifth Amendment on the territorial jurisdiction of federal courts may be, this statute does not transgress them. Rather, this act represents a considered judgment on a sensitive matter of foreign policy combating international terrorism that is within the prerogative of the political branches. The act is also appropriately limited and scoped to that end as it only triggers jurisdiction if the PLO or the PA engaged in certain enumerated conduct and only for ATA suits. In that sense, the statute puts the PLO and the PA on full unambiguous notice that they could be subject to personal jurisdiction in suits like the ones before us. In addition, even if the Fifth Amendment, like the Fourteenth Amendment requires an evaluation of whether the assertion of jurisdiction is reasonable, in this particular case, the Promoting Security and Justice Act easily passes muster under the factors we have looked to in evaluating fairness to the defendant under the Fourteenth Amendment. Indeed, the PLO and the PA do not complain that litigating these cases in the United States would be an unfair burden. Accordingly, we hold that the Promoting Security and Justice Acts provision for personal jurisdiction comports with the due process clause of the Fifth Amendment. The judgments of the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit are reversed and the cases are remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment in which Justice Gorsuch joined as to part two.
